questions.question: Needle chest decompression converts a tension pneumothorax into:
questions.rationale: Releasing trapped air relieves the tension and leaves a simple pneumothorax, restoring venous return.

questions.question: The anterior decompression landmark is the:
questions.rationale: The traditional anterior site is the 2nd–3rd intercostal space at the midclavicular line.

questions.question: The lateral decompression landmark is the:
options: 4th or 5th ICS at the anterior axillary line
questions.rationale: The lateral approach uses the 4th–5th intercostal space at the anterior axillary line.

questions.question: The needle is inserted just over the TOP of the rib to avoid:
options: The intercostal neurovascular bundle on the rib's underside
questions.rationale: The nerve, artery, and vein run along the inferior margin of each rib; entering over the top avoids them.

questions.question: A recommended anterior decompression catheter is approximately:
questions.rationale: A large-bore (about 14 gauge/8 French) catheter 6–9 cm long is recommended to reliably reach the pleural space.

questions.question: Which is an indication for emergency chest decompression?
options: Tension pneumothorax signs plus loss of radial pulse
Stable vitals with a small simple pneumothorax
questions.rationale: Decompress with tension signs plus more than one of distress/cyanosis, lost radial pulse, or decreasing LOC.

questions.question: A common cause of failed anterior needle decompression is:
options: A catheter too short to reach the pleural space
questions.rationale: Studies show many standard catheters are too short for the anterior chest wall, causing failure.

questions.question: A key advantage of the lateral approach in tactical care is:
options: It can be done without removing body armor
It is the only sterile option
questions.rationale: The lateral site allows decompression without removing body armor and may more reliably reach the pleura.

questions.question: Successful decompression is suggested by:
options: An audible rush of air and clinical improvement
questions.rationale: A rush of air and improvement (better breathing, returning pulse, improved LOC) indicate success.

questions.question: Needle decompression will NOT help which condition?
options: Air trapped in the pleural space
questions.rationale: Decompression releases air, not blood; a hemothorax requires shock care and surgical management.

questions.question: After decompression you should:
options: Secure the catheter and monitor for re-accumulation
questions.rationale: Decompression is temporary; secure the catheter, reassess frequently, and repeat if tension recurs.

questions.question: Definitive treatment of a tension pneumothorax is:
options: A chest tube (tube thoracostomy) at the hospital
questions.rationale: Needle decompression is a bridge; the chest tube provides definitive drainage.

questions.question: Prehospital ultrasound can assist by:
options: Confirming a pneumothorax before the procedure
questions.rationale: Ultrasound improves diagnostic accuracy by confirming the pneumothorax before needle placement.

questions.question: Are decompression indications different in tactical versus civilian environments?
options: No, the indications are the same
questions.rationale: The indications for needle decompression are identical regardless of the care environment.

questions.question: A complication of placing the needle below the rib margin is:
options: Laceration of the intercostal nerve, artery, or vein
questions.rationale: The neurovascular bundle lies under the rib; entry below the rib can lacerate these structures.

questions.question: The catheter direction during insertion should be:
options: Perpendicular to the chest wall at the landmark
questions.rationale: Insert perpendicular to the chest wall at the chosen interspace, over the top of the rib, into the pleural space.

questions.question: A theoretical benefit of the 3rd intercostal lateral site is:
options: Less risk of injuring the liver or spleen
questions.rationale: A higher lateral site reduces the risk of solid-organ injury and may be more quickly accessible in combat.

questions.question: If the patient re-deteriorates after an initially successful decompression, you should:
options: Assume the device failed permanently and stop
Reassess and repeat decompression for re-accumulation
questions.rationale: A re-tensioning pneumothorax (kinked/clotted catheter) requires reassessment and repeat decompression.
